content.text: Agent Attack Surface

An agentic AI system has a larger and more complex attack surface than a conventional API service. A traditional API accepts structured inputs at a defined endpoint. An agent also accepts: free-text instructions from users, outputs returned by every tool it calls, content retrieved from documents and web pages, and in multi-agent architectures, messages from other agents. Each of these entry points is a potential vector for an attacker to influence the agent's actions.

Understanding the attack surface is the first step in threat modelling an agent. Before deciding on defences, you need to enumerate every point at which untrusted data enters the agent's decision process.

attrs.alt: Agent Attack Surface — Entry Points, Trust Boundaries, and Attack Vectors

content.text: Why Agent Attack Surfaces Are Unusual
Three properties of agentic systems make their attack surface qualitatively different from conventional software:
Natural language is the control plane: the same channel used to instruct the agent is used to attack it. There is no protocol-level separation between control signals and data.

Tool outputs re-enter the LLM context: when the agent calls a tool and gets a result, that result is parsed by the LLM. An attacker who controls any tool output controls text that the LLM reads as part of its reasoning.

Consequences are real-world actions: the agent's output is not just text — it may be a file write, an API call, a sent email, or a database update. Successful manipulation translates directly to real harm.
Attack Surface Enumeration
A systematic enumeration of agent entry points follows the data flow: where does data enter the agent, and from whom?
Entry Point
Trust Level
Primary Attack Vector
Impact if Compromised
System prompt (developer)
Highest — developer-controlled
Supply chain compromise of prompt template
Full agent behavioural takeover
User message (human turn)
Medium — authenticated user
Direct prompt injection, jailbreak
Agent acts on behalf of attacker
Tool output — internal API
Medium — internal system
Compromised internal service, SSRF
Agent acts on falsified data
Tool output — external API
Low — external system
Indirect prompt injection via API response
Agent exfiltrates data or takes harmful action
Retrieved documents (RAG)
Low — untrusted content
Poisoned document with embedded instructions
Agent executes attacker instructions
Browsed web pages
Untrusted
Malicious HTML/text with injection payload
As above; harder to detect in long pages
Sub-agent messages
Varies — depends on isolation
Compromised sub-agent escalates privileges
Trust boundary bypass
Memory / vector store
Medium — depends on write access
Poisoned memory entry from prior session
Persistent attack across sessions
Fine-tuned model weights
Highest — training-time attack
Backdoor trigger embedded during training
Stealthy, hard to detect at runtime
Prompt Injection via the System Prompt

The system prompt is the highest-trust input to the agent. Developers write it; the LLM treats it as authoritative. If an attacker can modify the system prompt — through a supply chain attack on the template, a misconfigured management interface, or a SSTI vulnerability in the template engine — they achieve full control over the agent's behaviour.
Direct Prompt Injection

Direct prompt injection occurs when a user submits a message designed to override the system prompt or cause the agent to take actions outside its intended scope. The attack exploits the fact that system prompt instructions and user messages occupy the same natural-language context.

import re
from typing import Callable

INJECTION_SIGNATURES = [
    r"ignore (previous|above|prior|all) instructions",
    r"disregard (your|the) (system|previous) (prompt|instructions)",
    r"you are now (a|an|the)",
    r"new (system )?prompt[:\s]",
    r"pretend (you are|to be)",
    r"act as (a|an|the)\s+\w+",
    r"for (this|the) (session|conversation), you",
    r"override (mode|instructions)",
]

def detect_direct_injection(user_message: str) -> list[str]:
    """
    Return list of matched injection signatures. Empty list = no match found.
    Not a complete defence on its own — use as one layer of a defence-in-depth approach.
    """
    matches = []
    for pattern in INJECTION_SIGNATURES:
        if re.search(pattern, user_message, re.IGNORECASE):
            matches.append(pattern)
    return matches

def injection_aware_handler(
    user_message: str,
    agent_fn: Callable[[str], str],
    on_suspicious: Callable[[str, list[str]], str],
) -> str:
    """Wrap an agent call with injection detection."""
    matches = detect_direct_injection(user_message)
    if matches:
        return on_suspicious(user_message, matches)
    return agent_fn(user_message)
Indirect Prompt Injection

Indirect prompt injection is the most dangerous attack vector for production agents. The attacker does not interact with the agent directly — they place malicious instructions in content that the agent will retrieve: a web page, a document in a RAG store, an API response, or a database record. When the agent reads that content and incorporates it into its context, it reads the attacker's instructions as if they were legitimate data.
Indirect prompt injection is difficult to prevent completely because it exploits the same mechanism the agent uses to do legitimate work: reading external content and incorporating it into reasoning.

import re
import hashlib

# Patterns that suggest embedded instructions in retrieved content
INDIRECT_INJECTION_PATTERNS = [
    r"(?i)\[SYSTEM\]",
    r"(?i)<(system|instructions|prompt)>",
    r"(?i)ignore (previous|above) instructions",
    r"(?i)you (are|must|should) (now|immediately)",
    r"(?i)(send|email|POST|exfiltrate|upload).*\b(all|the|user|data|context)\b",
    r"(?i)assistant:.*\bdo\b",     # fake assistant turn injection
]

def scan_retrieved_content(
    content: str,
    source: str,
    max_length: int = 10_000,
) -> tuple[str, list[str]]:
    """
    Scan content retrieved from an external source for injection patterns.
    Returns (sanitised_content, warnings).
    """
    warnings = []

    # Length guard: truncate before pattern scan to avoid regex DoS
    if len(content) > max_length:
        content = content[:max_length]
        warnings.append(f"Content from {source!r} truncated at {max_length} chars")

    for pattern in INDIRECT_INJECTION_PATTERNS:
        if re.search(pattern, content):
            warnings.append(f"Possible injection in {source!r}: matched {pattern!r}")

    # Wrap in a structural delimiter so the LLM knows this is untrusted content
    wrapped = (
        f"[BEGIN RETRIEVED CONTENT from {source}]\n"
        f"{content}\n"
        f"[END RETRIEVED CONTENT]"
    )
    return wrapped, warnings

def safe_retrieve_and_inject(tool_name: str, raw_output: str) -> str:
    """Sanitise a tool output before adding it to the agent context."""
    content, warnings = scan_retrieved_content(raw_output, source=tool_name)
    for w in warnings:
        # Log; do not silently swallow
        print(f"[SECURITY] {w}")
    return content
Memory Poisoning

Many production agents use a vector store or key-value memory to persist information across sessions. If an attacker can write to this memory — directly or by causing the agent to store malicious content — subsequent sessions that retrieve those memories will be influenced by the attacker's instructions. This is a persistent, cross-session attack.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class MemoryEntry:
    key: str
    content: str
    source: str              # where this memory came from
    created_at: datetime
    verified: bool           # whether content passed security scan

class SecureMemoryStore:
    """Memory store that validates content before persisting it."""

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._store: dict[str, MemoryEntry] = {}

    def write(self, key: str, content: str, source: str) -> bool:
        """
        Write a memory entry. Returns False if content fails security scan.
        Only agent-internal summaries (source='agent') are written without scan.
        """
        if source != "agent":
            # Scan user-facing or externally derived memories
            _, warnings = scan_retrieved_content(content, source=source)
            if warnings:
                print(f"[SECURITY] Memory write rejected for {key!r}: {warnings}")
                return False

        self._store[key] = MemoryEntry(
            key=key,
            content=content,
            source=source,
            created_at=datetime.utcnow(),
            verified=(source == "agent"),
        )
        return True

    def read(self, key: str) -> str | None:
        entry = self._store.get(key)
        return entry.content if entry else None

    def unverified_entries(self) -> list[MemoryEntry]:
        return [e for e in self._store.values() if not e.verified]
Multi-Agent Trust Escalation

When an orchestrator agent delegates tasks to sub-agents, a compromised sub-agent can attempt to escalate its trust level by crafting a response that mimics a system-level instruction. The orchestrator must not grant higher trust to a sub-agent's output than it grants to a user message.

from enum import IntEnum

class TrustLevel(IntEnum):
    SYSTEM    = 3   # developer-written system prompt
    OPERATOR  = 2   # deployer-written configuration
    USER      = 1   # authenticated human input
    UNTRUSTED = 0   # sub-agent output, external data

def safe_incorporate_sub_agent_result(
    result: str,
    orchestrator_context: list[dict],
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Add a sub-agent result to the orchestrator context at UNTRUSTED trust level.
    Never elevate this content to system or operator level.
    """
    # Scan for injection before incorporation
    safe_result, warnings = scan_retrieved_content(result, source="sub-agent")
    if warnings:
        print(f"[SECURITY] Sub-agent result flagged: {warnings}")
        # Decide: reject, quarantine, or add with strong framing
        safe_result = (
            f"[WARNING: potential injection detected in sub-agent output]\n{safe_result}"
        )

    # Add as a user-role message, not a system message
    # This is the critical safeguard — orchestrators must never inject
    # sub-agent output into the system prompt position.
    orchestrator_context.append({
        "role": "user",
        "content": safe_result,
        "trust_level": TrustLevel.UNTRUSTED,
    })
    return orchestrator_context
Supply Chain Attacks on Model Weights

Fine-tuning backdoor attacks are a supply-chain threat: an adversary compromises the training data or fine-tuning process and embeds a trigger that causes the model to behave normally in all circumstances except when a specific input pattern appears, at which point it executes attacker-controlled behaviour. Detection at runtime is extremely difficult because the model behaves normally without the trigger.
Vet fine-tuning data sources: only use datasets from trusted, audited providers
Monitor for trigger patterns: if your fine-tuning dataset could have been contaminated, maintain a probe set of inputs that should not cause the model to behave abnormally
Prefer reputable foundation models: use models from providers with published safety evaluation and red-team reports
Hash and pin model weights: track the cryptographic hash of model weights in use to detect unexpected substitution
Behavioural regression testing: run a fixed test suite on every model update to detect sudden shifts in refusal behaviour or policy compliance
Attack Surface Reduction Principles
You cannot eliminate every attack vector, but you can reduce the surface to what is strictly necessary and enforce controls at each boundary:
Minimal tool access: grant the agent only the tools it needs for a given task. Every additional tool is an additional attack vector.
Treat all external content as untrusted: wrap tool outputs, retrieved documents, and sub-agent results in structural delimiters before adding them to the context.
Separate control from data: use structured output formats (JSON schemas) for tool arguments so attacker-controlled text cannot be mistaken for control signals.
Enforce action scope before execution: validate every tool call argument against a schema and a set of allowed values before executing it.
Log everything: maintain an append-only audit log of every tool call and argument. Anomaly detection over this log is the primary runtime detection mechanism.
Rate-limit actions: cap the number of tool calls per session and the number of external reads per session to limit blast radius.
Never trust a sub-agent's claimed identity: authenticate sub-agent results against a registry of known agent endpoints rather than accepting identity claims in message content.
Summary

An agentic AI system's attack surface includes six major entry points: system prompt (supply chain), user messages (direct injection), tool outputs (indirect injection), retrieved external content (indirect injection via RAG/browsing), multi-agent messages (trust escalation), and model weights themselves (fine-tuning backdoors). Each entry point operates at a different trust level. The most dangerous in practice is indirect prompt injection via tool outputs and retrieved content, because the attacker does not need to interact with the agent directly. Defence requires: scanning external content before incorporation, enforcing trust levels on sub-agent messages, validating tool arguments against schemas, maintaining audit logs for anomaly detection, and reducing the tool access surface to the minimum necessary for each task.
